topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The U-Haul was finally parked in the driveway, a monument to a decision made with excessive force. Sarah stood on the porch, hands clenched so tightly her knuckles were white, watching her parents approach the car with a mixture of hesitant warmth and undeniable expectation. "Well, this is it," she managed, the words tasting like ash in her mouth. Inside, she felt a rigid tension vibrating through her spine, a refusal to yield to the gravity of the situation. When her mother offered a hug, Sarah managed a brief, stiff squeeze before stepping back, creating a deliberate, almost visible distance. "I've thought this through," she announced, her voice pitched a little too high, a shield against the sudden, overwhelming urge to retreat into the familiar comfort of her own solitude. She spent the first evening meticulously arranging her few boxes, every placement precise, a silent declaration that her space, her routine, was not up for negotiation.

[story 2]

I paced the living room floor, the worn rug feeling suddenly too small for the volume of my internal resistance. Every suggestion they made—about dinner plans, about setting up the Wi-Fi, about just *being*—felt like a series of small, irritating pebbles thrown against a dam. I kept my gaze fixed on the window, watching the streetlights flicker on, refusing to meet my father’s questioning look. "We can try to make things work," he said, his tone gentle but firm, the kind of tone that always signaled an attempt to smooth over the friction. I didn't turn around. Instead, I picked up a book, the spine digging into my palm, and focused intently on the dense text, letting the silence between us stretch thin and brittle. I needed this space, this controlled environment, and I wasn't going to let anyone chip away at the perimeter.

[story 3]

The air in the kitchen felt thick, saturated with unspoken expectations. When my mother started talking about shared recipes and weekend activities, a tightness began to constrict my chest. I nodded politely, offering vague affirmations, but my mind was already charting escape routes. I moved through the house with a focused, almost mechanical efficiency, performing necessary tasks without engaging in the pleasantries. When my father asked if I wanted to watch a movie, I shook my head once, a small, sharp motion that conveyed a definitive 'no' without uttering a single word. The effort it took to maintain this outward composure was exhausting; every interaction felt like navigating a minefield, and I was determined to walk around it without ever letting my footing slip.
